analysis:
Across the top heuristics the common, effective structure is identical: (1) Pareto-prefilter, (2) cheap vectorized proxy scoring (box-volume ± few-weight scalarizations) to produce an O(k)-sized shortlist, (3) a greedy/CELF lazy-greedy stage that uses cached singleton HVs (and selective pairwise caches when the shortlist is tiny), and (4) a bounded 1-for-1 local-swap cleanup. The stronger variants (Heuristics 1–3) tune three levers well: deterministic shortlist sizing, aggressive caching, and careful lazy evaluation to minimize exact HV calls.

Frequent inefficiencies in weaker variants:
- Excessive exact HV evaluations (recomputing full vstack HVs inside inner loops) or overly generous pairwise caching that explodes as shortlist grows.
- Python-level membership and repeated np.vstack operations (list membership checks, rebuilding arrays) cause avoidable O(N^2) overhead.
- Non-vectorized or naive nondominated filters and repeated computation of the same proxies or diffs.
- Large, undisciplined HV-call budgets that trade CPU for marginal HV gains.

Good patterns to preserve:
- CELF/lazy timestamps with singleton HV cache—this cuts HV calls dramatically.
- Deterministic seeding and stable tie-breakers for reproducibility.
- Shortlist-size tied to k and Pareto-front size (not a fixed constant).
- Early, cheap upper-bound pruning (box-volume or singleton HV) before any HV recompute.

Low-cost improvements seen in stronger heuristics:
- Use lexsort/argsort on vectorized scores to build shortlists deterministically and cheaply.
- Keep selected membership as a set for O(1) checks and maintain selected_points as a preallocated array where possible to avoid repeated stacking.
- Limit pairwise HV caches to small shortlists (≤ ~30–40); otherwise rely on singleton caches + lazy recomputation.
- Constrain local-swap exploration (cap iterations and candidate set) and restart CELF after a successful swap to ensure correctness with few HV calls.

Trade-offs to monitor:
- More weight vectors improve HVC approximations but are only useful if shortlist size and HV budget are controlled; otherwise cost outweighs benefit.
- Larger HV budgets sometimes improve final HV but give diminishing returns and increase runtime variance.
experience: Design heuristics that (1) vectorize all cheap proxies and nondominated filtering, (2) adapt shortlist size to k and true Pareto size, (3) use CELF lazy greedy with singleton cache + tight box upper bounds, (4) restrict pairwise caching to tiny shortlists, and (5) limit swap candidates/iterations. Measure and cap exact-HV calls; prefer deterministic sets/arrays to avoid Python-level membership and repeated vstack overhead.
current_reflection: Design heuristics that (1) vectorize all cheap proxies and nondominated filtering, (2) adapt shortlist size to k and true Pareto size, (3) use CELF lazy greedy with singleton cache + tight box upper bounds, (4) restrict pairwise caching to tiny shortlists, and (5) limit swap candidates/iterations. Measure and cap exact-HV calls; prefer deterministic sets/arrays to avoid Python-level membership and repeated vstack overhead.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.

Design next heuristics by enforcing an O(k) shortlist, always cache singleton HVs, use CELF lazy evaluation with timestamps, apply cheap box-volume upper bounds to prune candidates, enable pairwise caching only for very small shortlists, cap HV calls and swap iterations, and prefer deterministic stable ordering. These focused changes yield most HV-quality gain per expensive HV call.

Prioritize reducing exact HV calls: build a high-precision O(k) shortlist with cheap proxies, cache singleton (and small pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates and box‑bound pruning, then run a tiny deterministic swap pass. Add an explicit HV-call budget with graceful fallback approximations and vectorized nondomination/precomputation to get consistent, fast, and high-quality k‑subsets.
bad_reflections: Favor a pipeline that (1) vectorizes cheap proxies to form an adaptive O(k) shortlist, (2) precompute singleton HVs, (3) run CELF lazy greedy with timestamps and cheap upper‑bound pruning, (4) limit exact HV calls with an explicit budget and selective pairwise cache, and (5) finish with a tiny bounded swap. Optimize data structures (sets, arrays) and avoid repeated full HV recomputations.

Design pipelines that (1) Pareto-filter, (2) rank with vectorized box+scalar proxies to build an O(k) shortlist, (3) precompute singleton HVs, (4) run CELF lazy greedy with tight upper bounds and an HV-call budget, then (5) do a small bounded swap pass. Cache pairwise HVs only when shortlist ≤ ~40 and keep all steps deterministic.

Design deterministically. Pareto‑prefilter, score with box+adaptive scalar probes, make shortlist O(k) (scale with pareto size), use CELF lazy greedy with singleton HV cache and an explicit HV‑call budget, prune with box-volume upper bounds, avoid pairwise caches when shortlist>~40, use sets/masks for membership, minimize vstacking by reusing buffers, and do a tiny, targeted swap pass on a small candidate pool.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: vectorized proxies, nondominated filtering, adaptive O(k) shortlist, CELF lazy greedy, singleton HVC cache, box upper bounds, pairwise cache cutoff (~40), HV-call budget, deterministic arrays.
- Advice: Vectorize prefiltering and cheap proxies to build an adaptive O(k) shortlist (scale with k and Pareto size). Run CELF lazy greedy with singleton HVC cache and tight box-volume bounds; enable pairwise caching only for tiny shortlists (≤40). Cap exact-HV calls, run a tiny deterministic swap pass (limit candidates/iterations), and use arrays/stable ordering to avoid Python membership and vstack overhead.
- Avoid: Unbatched/nested HV recomputations, unbounded swap/HV loops, pairwise caching on large shortlists, Python-level sets/repeated vstacking, and nondeterministic ordering.
- Explanation: Minimizes costly exact-HV work while maximizing quality per HV call, yielding consistent, fast, memory‑efficient k‑subsets.
